ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for peanut toast as a Singaporean kopitiam item; closest parent is peanut butter sandwich (FOODON_03302397).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to peanut toast; mapped to bread and similar products (A000E) as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Traditional Peanut Toast consists of two slices of toasted white bread spread with peanut butter, weighing approximately 80 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Peanut butter sandwich on white bread — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 21155 and composite)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by combining USDA SR Legacy data for toasted white bread (NDB 18069) and smooth peanut butter (NDB 16098) in a ratio of approximately 60:40 by weight, consistent with a standard peanut toast preparation. This ratio reflects typical kopitiam-style peanut toast where two slices of bread (~48 g) are spread with approximately 32 g of peanut butter. Per-100g values are calculated from this composite. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a direct entry for traditional peanut toast as a standalone item; USDA SR Legacy is used as the best available approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean kopitiam peanut toast. GI of 62 is estimated using white bread as the dominant glycemic driver (GI ~70 per Foster-Powell et al. 2002, glucose reference) moderated by the significant fat and protein contribution of peanut butter, which is known to lower the overall meal GI. The composite GI of 62 is consistent with published values for peanut butter sandwiches on white bread (GI ~59–67, glucose reference) reported in the same table. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 34.8) / 100 ≈ 21.6, where carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 43.5 × 0.80 = 34.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~248 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful that a single serving of peanut toast contributes approximately 248 mg sodium, representing about 10% of the 2,400 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board; choosing low-sodium peanut butter and limiting portion size can help manage intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and moderate glycemic load (~21.6 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI and moderate glycemic load of peanut toast mean it can cause a meaningful postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to one slice, pair with a protein source, and prefer wholegrain bread to reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat from peanut butter with low saturated fat and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanut toast provides a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (monounsaturated 8.1 g and polyunsaturated 4.9 g versus saturated 3.5 g per 100 g) and zero dietary cholesterol, which is consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional Peanut Toast is a classic Singaporean kopitiam breakfast and snack item consisting of two slices of white bread that are toasted until golden and crisp, then generously spread with smooth or crunchy peanut butter. It is a staple of Singapore's coffee-shop culture, commonly served alongside a soft-boiled egg and a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea). The bread provides a firm, crunchy exterior with a soft interior, while the peanut butter contributes a rich, nutty, slightly sweet flavour and a creamy texture. Nutritionally, peanut toast is a moderate-energy food (~370 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~11 g per 100 g) from the peanut butter, predominantly unsaturated fats (~13 g per 100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (~43.5 g per 100 g) from the bread. The glycemic index is approximately 62 (medium), making it a food that can raise blood glucose moderately. Sodium content is moderate (~310 mg per 100 g), primarily from the bread and peanut butter. It is naturally free of cholesterol and dairy. Clinically, it is suitable for most healthy adults in moderate portions but warrants attention for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium GI and glycemic load, and for those with hypertension due to its sodium content. The dish is also known as Roti Bakar Kacang in Malay and 花生吐司 in Chinese, and is widely available at Toast Box, Ya Kun Kaya Toast, and similar kopitiam chains across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in peanut toast?
What is the GI of peanut toast?
Is peanut toast high in sodium?
What is traditional peanut toast in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for Traditional Peanut Toast as a standalone kopitiam item. Nutrient values are derived from a defensible USDA SR Legacy composite of toasted white bread and smooth peanut butter in a realistic preparation ratio. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in a peer-reviewed GI table. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are adapted rather than directly measured for this specific product.
notes: Nutrient values are calculated from a composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for toasted white bread (NDB 18069) and smooth peanut butter (NDB 16098) at a 60:40 weight ratio. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the composite source entries and cannot be reliably calculated without direct measurement. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for bread or peanut butter. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as modern commercial peanut butter and white bread in Singapore are formulated to contain negligible or no trans fats; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA SR Legacy data. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as both white bread and peanut butter are plant-derived and contain no dietary cholesterol; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 as neither bread nor peanut butter contains meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on Foster-Powell et al. (2002) AJCN peanut butter sandwich on white bread data. Glycemic load per serving (80 g) = (62 × 34.8) / 100 ≈ 21.6. Served at Toast Box - Funan per field survey data. Price SGD 3.30 per field survey data listing.
